{
  "markdown": "ONNX Export for YOLO26 Models - Ultralytics YOLO Docs\n\nSkip to content\n\n[](https://github.com/ultralytics/ultralytics/tree/main/docs/en/integrations/onnx.md) [](javascript:void(0))\n\n# ONNX Export for YOLO26 Models\n\n~43% faster inference.\n\n- Exporting the Ultralytics YOLO26 model to ONNX can deliver up to a 43% boost in inference speed, enabling faster and more efficient deployment.\n\nOften, when deploying [computer vision](https://www.ultralytics.com/glossary/computer-vision-cv) models, you'll need a model format that's both flexible and compatible with multiple platforms.\n\nExporting [Ultralytics YOLO26](https://github.com/ultralytics/ultralytics) models to ONNX format streamlines deployment and ensures optimal performance across various environments. This guide will show you how to easily convert your YOLO26 models to ONNX and enhance their scalability and effectiveness in real-world applications.\n\nWatch: Ultralytics YOLO26 vs Ultralytics YOLO11 ONNX Inference Test | ~43% Faster Inference with YOLO26 🚀\n\n## ONNX and ONNX Runtime\n\n[ONNX](https://onnx.ai/), which stands for Open [Neural Network](https://www.ultralytics.com/glossary/neural-network-nn) Exchange, is a community project that Facebook and Microsoft initially developed. The ongoing development of ONNX is a collaborative effort supported by various organizations like IBM, Amazon (through AWS), and Google. The project aims to create an open file format designed to represent [machine learning](https://www.ultralytics.com/glossary/machine-learning-ml) models in a way that allows them to be used across different AI frameworks and hardware.\n\nONNX models can be used to transition between different frameworks seamlessly. For instance, a [deep learning](https://www.ultralytics.com/glossary/deep-learning-dl) model trained in PyTorch can be exported to ONNX format and then easily imported into TensorFlow.\n\nAlternatively, ONNX models can be used with ONNX Runtime. [ONNX Runtime](https://onnxruntime.ai/) is a versatile cross-platform accelerator for machine learning models that is compatible with frameworks like PyTorch, [TensorFlow](https://www.ultralytics.com/glossary/tensorflow), TFLite, scikit-learn, etc.\n\nONNX Runtime optimizes the execution of ONNX models by leveraging hardware-specific capabilities. This optimization allows the models to run efficiently and with high performance on various hardware platforms, including CPUs, GPUs, and specialized accelerators.\n\nWhether used independently or in tandem with ONNX Runtime, ONNX provides a flexible solution for machine learning [model deployment](https://www.ultralytics.com/glossary/model-deployment) and compatibility.\n\n## Key Features of ONNX Models\n\nThe ability of ONNX to handle various formats can be attributed to the following key features:\n\nCommon Model Representation: ONNX defines a common set of operators (like convolutions, layers, etc.) and a standard data format. When a model is converted to ONNX format, its architecture and weights are translated into this common representation. This uniformity ensures that the model can be understood by any framework that supports ONNX.\n\nVersioning and Backward Compatibility: ONNX maintains a versioning system for its operators. This ensures that even as the standard evolves, models created in older versions remain usable. Backward compatibility is a crucial feature that prevents models from becoming obsolete quickly.\n\nGraph-based Model Representation: ONNX represents models as computational graphs. This graph-based structure is a universal way of representing machine learning models, where nodes represent operations or computations, and edges represent the tensors flowing between them. This format is easily adaptable to various frameworks which also represent models as graphs.\n\nTools and Ecosystem: There is a rich ecosystem of tools around ONNX that assist in model conversion, visualization, and optimization. These tools make it easier for developers to work with ONNX models and to convert models between different frameworks seamlessly.\n\n## Common Usage of ONNX\n\nBefore we jump into how to export YOLO26 models to the ONNX format, let's take a look at where ONNX models are usually used.\n\n### CPU Deployment\n\nONNX models are often deployed on CPUs due to their compatibility with ONNX Runtime. This runtime is optimized for CPU execution. It significantly improves inference speed and makes real-time CPU deployments feasible.\n\n### Supported Deployment Options\n\nWhile ONNX models are commonly used on CPUs, they can also be deployed on the following platforms:\n\nGPU Acceleration: ONNX fully supports GPU acceleration, particularly NVIDIA CUDA. This enables efficient execution on NVIDIA GPUs for tasks that demand high computational power.\n\nEdge and Mobile Devices: ONNX extends to edge and mobile devices, perfect for on-device and real-time inference scenarios. It's lightweight and compatible with edge hardware.\n\nWeb Browsers: ONNX can run directly in web browsers, powering interactive and dynamic web-based AI applications.\n\n## Exporting YOLO26 Models to ONNX\n\nYou can expand model compatibility and deployment flexibility by converting YOLO26 models to ONNX format. [Ultralytics YOLO26](https://docs.ultralytics.com/models/yolo26/) provides a straightforward export process that can significantly enhance your model's performance across different platforms.\n\n### Installation\n\nTo install the required package, run:\n\nInstallation\n\nCLI\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n# Install the required package for YOLO26\npip install ultralytics\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nFor detailed instructions and best practices related to the installation process, check our [YOLO26 Installation guide](https://docs.ultralytics.com/quickstart/). While installing the required packages for YOLO26, if you encounter any difficulties, consult our [Common Issues guide](https://docs.ultralytics.com/guides/yolo-common-issues/) for solutions and tips.\n\n### Usage\n\nBefore diving into the usage instructions, be sure to check out the range of [YOLO26 models offered by Ultralytics](https://docs.ultralytics.com/models/). This will help you choose the most appropriate model for your project requirements.\n\nUsage\n\nPythonCLI\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nfrom ultralytics import YOLO\n\n# Load the YOLO26 model\nmodel = YOLO(\"yolo26n.pt\")\n\n# Export the model to ONNX format\nmodel.export(format=\"onnx\")  # creates 'yolo26n.onnx'\n\n# Load the exported ONNX model\nonnx_model = YOLO(\"yolo26n.onnx\")\n\n# Run inference\nresults = onnx_model(\"https://ultralytics.com/images/bus.jpg\")\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n# Export a YOLO26n PyTorch model to ONNX format\nyolo export model=yolo26n.pt format=onnx # creates 'yolo26n.onnx'\n\n# Run inference with the exported model\nyolo predict model=yolo26n.onnx source='https://ultralytics.com/images/bus.jpg'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Export Arguments\n\nWhen exporting your YOLO26 model to ONNX format, you can customize the process using various arguments to optimize for your specific deployment needs:\n\n| Argument | Type | Default | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| &#96;format&#96; | &#96;str&#96; | &#96;'onnx'&#96; | Target format for the exported model, defining compatibility with various deployment environments. |\n| &#96;imgsz&#96; | &#96;int&#96; or&#96;tuple&#96; | &#96;640&#96; | Desired image size for the model input. Can be an integer for square images or a tuple&#96;(height, width)&#96; for specific dimensions. |\n| &#96;half&#96; | &#96;bool&#96; | &#96;False&#96; | Enables FP16 (half-precision) quantization, reducing model size and potentially speeding up inference on supported hardware. |\n| &#96;dynamic&#96; | &#96;bool&#96; | &#96;False&#96; | Allows dynamic input sizes, enhancing flexibility in handling varying image dimensions. |\n| &#96;simplify&#96; | &#96;bool&#96; | &#96;True&#96; | Simplifies the model graph with&#96;onnxslim&#96;, potentially improving performance and compatibility. |\n| &#96;opset&#96; | &#96;int&#96; | &#96;None&#96; | Specifies the ONNX opset version for compatibility with different ONNX parsers and runtimes. If not set, uses the latest supported version. |\n| &#96;nms&#96; | &#96;bool&#96; | &#96;False&#96; | Adds Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS), essential for accurate and efficient detection post-processing. |\n| &#96;batch&#96; | &#96;int&#96; | &#96;1&#96; | Specifies export model batch inference size or the max number of images the exported model will process concurrently in&#96;predict&#96; mode. |\n| &#96;device&#96; | &#96;str&#96; | &#96;None&#96; | Specifies the device for exporting: GPU (&#96;device=0&#96;), CPU (&#96;device=cpu&#96;), MPS for Apple silicon (&#96;device=mps&#96;). |\n\nFor more details about the export process, visit the [Ultralytics documentation page on exporting](https://docs.ultralytics.com/modes/export/).\n\n## Deploying Exported YOLO26 ONNX Models\n\nOnce you've successfully exported your Ultralytics YOLO26 models to ONNX format, the next step is deploying these models in various environments. For detailed instructions on deploying your ONNX models, take a look at the following resources:\n\n[ONNX Runtime Python API Documentation](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/api_summary.html): This guide provides essential information for loading and running ONNX models using ONNX Runtime.\n\n[Deploying on Edge Devices](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/tutorials/iot-edge/): Check out this docs page for different examples of deploying ONNX models on edge.\n\n[ONNX Tutorials on GitHub](https://github.com/onnx/tutorials): A collection of comprehensive tutorials that cover various aspects of using and implementing ONNX models in different scenarios.\n\n[Triton Inference Server](https://docs.ultralytics.com/guides/triton-inference-server/): Learn how to deploy your ONNX models with NVIDIA's Triton Inference Server for high-performance, scalable deployments.\n\n## Summary\n\nIn this guide, you've learned how to export Ultralytics YOLO26 models to ONNX format to increase their interoperability and performance across various platforms. You were also introduced to the ONNX Runtime and ONNX deployment options.\n\nONNX export is just one of many [export formats](https://docs.ultralytics.com/modes/export/) supported by Ultralytics YOLO26, allowing you to deploy your models in virtually any environment. Depending on your specific needs, you might also want to explore other export options like [TensorRT](https://docs.ultralytics.com/integrations/tensorrt/) for maximum GPU performance or [CoreML](https://docs.ultralytics.com/integrations/coreml/) for Apple devices.\n\nFor further details on usage, visit the [ONNX official documentation](https://onnx.ai/onnx/intro/).\n\nAlso, if you'd like to know more about other Ultralytics YOLO26 integrations, visit our [integration guide page](https://docs.ultralytics.com/integrations/). You'll find plenty of useful resources and insights there.\n\n## FAQ\n\n### How do I export YOLO26 models to ONNX format using Ultralytics?\n\nTo export your YOLO26 models to ONNX format using Ultralytics, follow these steps:\n\nUsage\n\nPythonCLI\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nfrom ultralytics import YOLO\n\n# Load the YOLO26 model\nmodel = YOLO(\"yolo26n.pt\")\n\n# Export the model to ONNX format\nmodel.export(format=\"onnx\")  # creates 'yolo26n.onnx'\n\n# Load the exported ONNX model\nonnx_model = YOLO(\"yolo26n.onnx\")\n\n# Run inference\nresults = onnx_model(\"https://ultralytics.com/images/bus.jpg\")\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n# Export a YOLO26n PyTorch model to ONNX format\nyolo export model=yolo26n.pt format=onnx # creates 'yolo26n.onnx'\n\n# Run inference with the exported model\nyolo predict model=yolo26n.onnx source='https://ultralytics.com/images/bus.jpg'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nFor more details, visit the [export documentation](https://docs.ultralytics.com/modes/export/).\n\n### What are the advantages of using ONNX Runtime for deploying YOLO26 models?\n\nUsing ONNX Runtime for deploying YOLO26 models offers several advantages:\n\n- Cross-platform compatibility: ONNX Runtime supports various platforms, such as Windows, macOS, and Linux, ensuring your models run smoothly across different environments.\n- Hardware acceleration: ONNX Runtime can leverage hardware-specific optimizations for CPUs, GPUs, and dedicated accelerators, providing high-performance inference.\n- Framework interoperability: Models trained in popular frameworks like [PyTorch](https://www.ultralytics.com/glossary/pytorch) or TensorFlow can be easily converted to ONNX format and run using ONNX Runtime.\n- Performance optimization: ONNX Runtime can provide up to 3x CPU speedup compared to native PyTorch models, making it ideal for deployment scenarios where GPU resources are limited.\n\nLearn more by checking the [ONNX Runtime documentation](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/api_summary.html).\n\n### What deployment options are available for YOLO26 models exported to ONNX?\n\nYOLO26 models exported to ONNX can be deployed on various platforms including:\n\n- CPUs: Utilizing ONNX Runtime for optimized CPU inference.\n- GPUs: Leveraging NVIDIA CUDA for high-performance GPU acceleration.\n- Edge devices: Running lightweight models on edge and mobile devices for real-time, on-device inference.\n- Web browsers: Executing models directly within web browsers for interactive web-based applications.\n- Cloud services: Deploying on cloud platforms that support ONNX format for scalable inference.\n\nFor more information, explore our guide on [model deployment options](https://docs.ultralytics.com/guides/model-deployment-options/).\n\n### Why should I use ONNX format for Ultralytics YOLO26 models?\n\nUsing ONNX format for Ultralytics YOLO26 models provides numerous benefits:\n\n- Interoperability: ONNX allows models to be transferred between different machine learning frameworks seamlessly.\n- Performance Optimization: ONNX Runtime can enhance model performance by utilizing hardware-specific optimizations.\n- Flexibility: ONNX supports various deployment environments, enabling you to use the same model on different platforms without modification.\n- Standardization: ONNX provides a standardized format that is widely supported across the industry, ensuring long-term compatibility.\n\nRefer to the comprehensive guide on [exporting YOLO26 models to ONNX](https://docs.ultralytics.com/integrations/onnx/).\n\n### How can I troubleshoot issues when exporting YOLO26 models to ONNX?\n\nWhen exporting YOLO26 models to ONNX, you might {
  "markdown": "API - Python API documentation\n\n# API#\n\n## API Overview#\n\nONNX Runtime loads and runs inference on a model in ONNX graph format, or ORT format (for memory and disk constrained environments).\n\nThe data consumed and produced by the model can be specified and accessed in the way that best matches your scenario.\n\n### Load and run a model#\n\nInferenceSession is the main class of ONNX Runtime. It is used to load and run an ONNX model, as well as specify environment and application configuration options.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nsession = onnxruntime.InferenceSession('model.onnx')\n\noutputs = session.run([output names], inputs)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nONNX and ORT format models consist of a graph of computations, modeled as operators, and implemented as optimized operator kernels for different hardware targets. ONNX Runtime orchestrates the execution of operator kernels via execution providers. An execution provider contains the set of kernels for a specific execution target (CPU, GPU, IoT etc). Execution provides are configured using the providers parameter. Kernels from different execution providers are chosen in the priority order given in the list of providers. In the example below if there is a kernel in the CUDA execution provider ONNX Runtime executes that on GPU. If not the kernel is executed on CPU.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nsession = onnxruntime.InferenceSession(\n        model, providers=['CUDAExecutionProvider', 'CPUExecutionProvider']\n)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe list of available execution providers can be found here: [Execution Providers](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/execution-providers).\n\nSince ONNX Runtime 1.10, you must explicitly specify the execution provider for your target. Running on CPU is the only time the API allows no explicit setting of the provider parameter. In the examples that follow, the CUDAExecutionProvider and CPUExecutionProvider are used, assuming the application is running on NVIDIA GPUs. Replace these with the execution provider specific to your environment.\n\nYou can supply other session configurations via the session options parameter. For example, to enable profiling on the session:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\noptions = onnxruntime.SessionOptions()\noptions.enable_profiling=True\nsession = onnxruntime.InferenceSession(\n        'model.onnx',\n        sess_options=options,\n        providers=['CUDAExecutionProvider', 'CPUExecutionProvider'])\n)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Data inputs and outputs#\n\nThe ONNX Runtime Inference Session consumes and produces data using its OrtValue class.\n\n#### Data on CPU#\n\nOn CPU (the default), OrtValues can be mapped to and from native Python data structures: numpy arrays, dictionaries and lists of numpy arrays.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n# X is numpy array on cpu\nortvalue = onnxruntime.OrtValue.ortvalue_from_numpy(X)\nortvalue.device_name()  # 'cpu'\nortvalue.shape()        # shape of the numpy array X\nortvalue.data_type()    # 'tensor(float)'\nortvalue.is_tensor()    # 'True'\nnp.array_equal(ortvalue.numpy(), X)  # 'True'\n\n# ortvalue can be provided as part of the input feed to a model\nsession = onnxruntime.InferenceSession(\n        'model.onnx',\n        providers=['CUDAExecutionProvider', 'CPUExecutionProvider'])\n)\nresults = session.run([\"Y\"], {\"X\": ortvalue})\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nBy default, ONNX Runtime always places input(s) and output(s) on CPU. Having the data on CPU may not optimal if the input or output is consumed and produced on a device other than CPU because it introduces data copy between CPU and the device.\n\n#### Data on device#\n\nONNX Runtime supports a custom data structure that supports all ONNX data formats that allows users to place the data backing these on a device, for example, on a CUDA supported device. In ONNX Runtime, this called IOBinding.\n\nTo use the IOBinding feature, replace InferenceSession.run() with InferenceSession.run_with_iobinding().\n\nA graph is executed on a device other than CPU, for instance CUDA. Users can use IOBinding to copy the data onto the GPU.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n# X is numpy array on cpu\nsession = onnxruntime.InferenceSession(\n        'model.onnx',\n        providers=['CUDAExecutionProvider', 'CPUExecutionProvider'])\n)\nio_binding = session.io_binding()\n# OnnxRuntime will copy the data over to the CUDA device if 'input' is consumed by nodes on the CUDA device\nio_binding.bind_cpu_input('input', X)\nio_binding.bind_output('output')\nsession.run_with_iobinding(io_binding)\nY = io_binding.copy_outputs_to_cpu()[0]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe input data is on a device, users directly use the input. The output data is on CPU.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n# X is numpy array on cpu\nX_ortvalue = onnxruntime.OrtValue.ortvalue_from_numpy(X, 'cuda', 0)\nsession = onnxruntime.InferenceSession(\n        'model.onnx',\n        providers=['CUDAExecutionProvider', 'CPUExecutionProvider'])\n)\nio_binding = session.io_binding()\nio_binding.bind_input(name='input', device_type=X_ortvalue.device_name(), device_id=0, element_type=np.float32, shape=X_ortvalue.shape(), buffer_ptr=X_ortvalue.data_ptr())\nio_binding.bind_output('output')\nsession.run_with_iobinding(io_binding)\nY = io_binding.copy_outputs_to_cpu()[0]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe input data and output data are both on a device, users directly use the input and also place output on the device.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#X is numpy array on cpu\nX_ortvalue = onnxruntime.OrtValue.ortvalue_from_numpy(X, 'cuda', 0)\nY_ortvalue = onnxruntime.OrtValue.ortvalue_from_shape_and_type([3, 2], np.float32, 'cuda', 0)  # Change the shape to the actual shape of the output being bound\nsession = onnxruntime.InferenceSession(\n        'model.onnx',\n        providers=['CUDAExecutionProvider', 'CPUExecutionProvider'])\n)\nio_binding = session.io_binding()\nio_binding.bind_input(\n        name='input',\n        device_type=X_ortvalue.device_name(),\n        device_id=0,\n        element_type=np.float32,\n        shape=X_ortvalue.shape(),\n        buffer_ptr=X_ortvalue.data_ptr()\n)\nio_binding.bind_output(\n        name='output',\n        device_type=Y_ortvalue.device_name(),\n        device_id=0,\n        element_type=np.float32,\n        shape=Y_ortvalue.shape(),\n        buffer_ptr=Y_ortvalue.data_ptr()\n)\nsession.run_with_iobinding(io_binding)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nUsers can request ONNX Runtime to allocate an output on a device. This is particularly useful for dynamic shaped outputs. Users can use the get_outputs() API to get access to the OrtValue (s) corresponding to the allocated output(s). Users can thus consume the ONNX Runtime allocated memory for the output as an OrtValue.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#X is numpy array on cpu\nX_ortvalue = onnxruntime.OrtValue.ortvalue_from_numpy(X, 'cuda', 0)\nsession = onnxruntime.InferenceSession(\n        'model.onnx',\n        providers=['CUDAExecutionProvider', 'CPUExecutionProvider'])\n)\nio_binding = session.io_binding()\nio_binding.bind_input(\n        name='input',\n        device_type=X_ortvalue.device_name(),\n        device_id=0,\n        element_type=np.float32,\n        shape=X_ortvalue.shape(),\n        buffer_ptr=X_ortvalue.data_ptr()\n)\n#Request ONNX Runtime to bind and allocate memory on CUDA for 'output'\nio_binding.bind_output('output', 'cuda')\nsession.run_with_iobinding(io_binding)\n# The following call returns an OrtValue which has data allocated by ONNX Runtime on CUDA\nort_output = io_binding.get_outputs()[0]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIn addition, ONNX Runtime supports directly working with OrtValue (s) while inferencing a model if provided as part of the input feed.\n\nUsers can bind OrtValue (s) directly.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n#X is numpy array on cpu\n#X is numpy array on cpu\nX_ortvalue = onnxruntime.OrtValue.ortvalue_from_numpy(X, 'cuda', 0)\nY_ortvalue = onnxruntime.OrtValue.ortvalue_from_shape_and_type([3, 2], np.float32, 'cuda', 0)  # Change the shape to the actual shape of the output being bound\nsession = onnxruntime.InferenceSession(\n        'model.onnx',\n        providers=['CUDAExecutionProvider', 'CPUExecutionProvider'])\n)\nio_binding = session.io_binding()\nio_binding.bind_ortvalue_input('input', X_ortvalue)\nio_binding.bind_ortvalue_output('output', Y_ortvalue)\nsession.run_with_iobinding(io_binding)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nYou can also bind inputs and outputs directly to a PyTorch tensor.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n# X is a PyTorch tensor on device\nsession = onnxruntime.InferenceSession('model.onnx', providers=['CUDAExecutionProvider', 'CPUExecutionProvider']))\nbinding = session.io_binding()\n\nX_tensor = X.contiguous()\n\nbinding.bind_input(\n    name='X',\n    device_type='cuda',\n    device_id=0,\n    element_type=np.float32,\n    shape=tuple(x_tensor.shape),\n    buffer_ptr=x_tensor.data_ptr(),\n    )\n\n## Allocate the PyTorch tensor for the model output\nY_shape = ... # You need to specify the output PyTorch tensor shape\nY_tensor = torch.empty(Y_shape, dtype=torch.float32, device='cuda:0').contiguous()\nbinding.bind_output(\n    name='Y',\n    device_type='cuda',\n    device_id=0,\n    element_type=np.float32,\n    shape=tuple(Y_tensor.shape),\n    buffer_ptr=Y_tensor.data_ptr(),\n)\n\nsession.run_with_iobinding(binding)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nYou can also see code examples of this API in in the [ONNX Runtime inferences examples](https://github.com/microsoft/onnxruntime-inference-examples/blob/main/python/api/onnxruntime-python-api.py).\n\nSome onnx data type (like TensorProto.BFLOAT16, TensorProto.FLOAT8E4M3FN and TensorProto.FLOAT8E5M2) are not supported by Numpy. You can directly bind input or output with Torch tensor of corresponding data type (like torch.bfloat16, torch.float8_e4m3fn and torch.float8_e5m2) in GPU memory.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nx = torch.ones([3], dtype=torch.float8_e5m2, device='cuda:0')\ny = torch.empty([3], dtype=torch.bfloat16, device='cuda:0')\n\nbinding = session.io_binding()\nbinding.bind_input(\n    name='X',\n    device_type='cuda',\n    device_id=0,\n    element_type=TensorProto.FLOAT8E5M2,\n    shape=tuple(x.shape),\n    buffer_ptr=x.data_ptr(),\n    )\nbinding.bind_output(\n    name='Y',\n    device_type='cuda',\n    device_id=0,\n    element_type=TensorProto.BFLOAT16,\n    shape=tuple(y.shape),\n    buffer_ptr=y.data_ptr(),\n    )\n    session.run_with_iobinding(binding)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## API Details#\n\n### InferenceSession#\n\nclass onnxruntime.InferenceSession(path_or_bytes: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)| [bytes](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#bytes)| [os.PathLike](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.PathLike), sess_options: onnxruntime.SessionOptions | [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)= None, providers: Sequence[[str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)| [tuple](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#tuple) [[str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str), [dict](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict) [Any, Any]]] | [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)= None, provider_options: Sequence[[dict](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict) [Any, Any]] | [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)= None, **kwargs) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#InferenceSession)#\n\nThis is the main class used to run a model.\n\nParameters:\n\npath_or_bytes – Filename or serialized ONNX or ORT format model in a byte string.\n\nsess_options – Session options.\n\nproviders – Optional sequence of providers in order of decreasing precedence. Values can either be provider names or tuples of (provider name, options dict). If not provided, then all available providers are used with the default precedence.\n\nprovider_options – Optional sequence of options dicts corresponding to the providers listed in ‘providers’.\n\nThe model type will be inferred unless explicitly set in the SessionOptions. To explicitly set:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nso = onnxruntime.SessionOptions()\n# so.add_session_config_entry('session.load_model_format', 'ONNX') or\nso.add_session_config_entry('session.load_model_format', 'ORT')\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nA file extension of ‘.ort’ will be inferred as an ORT format model. All other filenames are assumed to be ONNX format models.\n\n‘providers’ can contain either names or names and options. When any options are given in ‘providers’, ‘provider_options’ should not be used.\n\nThe list of providers is ordered by precedence. For example [‘CUDAExecutionProvider’, ‘CPUExecutionProvider’] means execute a node using CUDAExecutionProvider if capable, otherwise execute using CPUExecutionProvider.\n\ndisable_fallback()#\n\nDisable session.run() fallback mechanism.\n\nenable_fallback()#\n\nEnable session.Run() fallback mechanism. If session.Run() fails due to an internal Execution Provider failure, reset the Execution Providers enabled for this session. If GPU is enabled, fall back to CUDAExecutionProvider. otherwise fall back to CPUExecutionProvider.\n\nend_profiling()#\n\nEnd profiling and return results in a file.\n\nThe results are stored in a filename if the option&#96;onnxruntime.SessionOptions.enable_profiling()&#96;.\n\nget_inputs()#\n\nReturn the inputs metadata as a list of&#96;onnxruntime.NodeArg&#96;.\n\nget_modelmeta()#\n\nReturn the metadata. See&#96;onnxruntime.ModelMetadata&#96;.\n\nget_outputs()#\n\nReturn the outputs metadata as a list of&#96;onnxruntime.NodeArg&#96;.\n\nget_overridable_initializers()#\n\nReturn the inputs (including initializers) metadata as a list of&#96;onnxruntime.NodeArg&#96;.\n\nget_profiling_start_time_ns()#\n\nReturn the nanoseconds of profiling’s start time Comparable to time.monotonic_ns() after Python 3.3 On some platforms, this timer may not be as precise as nanoseconds For instance, on Windows and MacOS, the precision will be ~100ns\n\nget_provider_options()#\n\nReturn registered execution providers’ configurations.\n\nget_providers()#\n\nReturn list of registered execution providers.\n\nget_session_options()#\n\nReturn the session options. See&#96;onnxruntime.SessionOptions&#96;.\n\nio_binding()#\n\nReturn an onnxruntime.IOBinding object&#96;.\n\nrun(output_names, input_feed, run_options=None)#\n\nCompute the predictions.\n\nParameters:\n\noutput_names – name of the outputs\n\ninput_feed – dictionary&#96;{ input_name: input_value }&#96;\n\nrun_options – See&#96;onnxruntime.RunOptions&#96;.\n\nReturns:\n\nlist of results, every result is either a numpy array, a sparse tensor, a list or a dictionary.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nsess.run([output_name], {input_name: x})\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nrun_async(output_names, input_feed, callback, user_data, run_options=None)#\n\nCompute the predictions asynchronously in a separate cxx thread from ort intra-op threadpool.\n\nParameters:\n\noutput_names – name of the outputs\n\ninput_feed – dictionary&#96;{ input_name: input_value }&#96;\n\ncallback – python function that accept array of results, and a status string on error. The callback will be invoked by a cxx thread from ort intra-op threadpool.\n\nrun_options – See&#96;onnxruntime.RunOptions&#96;.\n\n:: class MyData: def __init__(self):\n\n# …\n\ndef save_results(self, results):\n\n# …\n\ndef callback(results: np.ndarray, user_data: MyData, err: str) -\u003e None: if err:\n\nprint (err)\n\nelse:\n\n# save results to user_data\n\nsess.run_async([output_name], {input_name: x}, callback)\n\nrun_with_iobinding(iobinding, run_options=None)#\n\nCompute the predictions.\n\nParameters:\n\niobinding – the iobinding object that has graph inputs/outputs bind.\n\nrun_options – See&#96;onnxruntime.RunOptions&#96;.\n\nrun_with_ort_values(output_names, input_dict_ort_values, run_options=None)#\n\nCompute the predictions.\n\nParameters:\n\noutput_names – name of the outputs\n\ninput_dict_ort_values – dictionary&#96;{ input_name: input_ort_value }&#96; See&#96;OrtValue&#96; class how to create OrtValue from numpy array or SparseTensor\n\nrun_options – See&#96;onnxruntime.RunOptions&#96;.\n\nReturns:\n\nan array of OrtValue\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nsess.run([output_name], {input_name: x})\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nrun_with_ortvaluevector(run_options, feed_names, feeds, fetch_names, fetches, fetch_devices)#\n\nCompute the predictions similar to other run_*() methods but with minimal C++/Python conversion overhead.\n\nParameters:\n\nrun_options – See&#96;onnxruntime.RunOptions&#96;.\n\nfeed_names – list of input names.\n\nfeeds – list of input OrtValue.\n\nfetch_names – list of output names.\n\nfetches – list of output OrtValue.\n\nfetch_devices – list of output devices.\n\nset_providers(providers=None, provider_options=None)#\n\nRegister the input list of execution providers. The underlying session is re-created.\n\nParameters:\n\nproviders – Optional sequence of providers in order of decreasing precedence. Values can either be provider names or tuples of (provider name, options dict). If not provided, then all available providers are used with the default precedence.\n\nprovider_options – Optional sequence of options dicts corresponding to the providers listed in ‘providers’.\n\n‘providers’ can contain either names or names and options. When any options are given in ‘providers’, ‘provider_options’ should not be used.\n\nThe list of providers is ordered by precedence. For example [‘CUDAExecutionProvider’, ‘CPUExecutionProvider’] means execute a node using CUDAExecutionProvider if capable, otherwise execute using CPUExecutionProvider.\n\n### Options#\n\n#### RunOptions#\n\nclass onnxruntime.RunOptions(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.RunOptions)#\n\nConfiguration information for a single Run.\n\nadd_run_config_entry(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.RunOptions, arg0: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str), arg1: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\nSet a single run configuration entry as a pair of strings.\n\nget_run_config_entry(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.RunOptions, arg0: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)) → [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)#\n\nGet a single run configuration value using the given configuration key.\n\nproperty log_severity_level#\n\nInfo, 2:Warning. 3:Error, 4:Fatal. Default is 2.\n\nType:\n\nLog severity level for a particular Run() invocation. 0\n\nType:\n\nVerbose, 1\n\nproperty log_verbosity_level#\n\nVLOG level if DEBUG build and run_log_severity_level is 0. Applies to a particular Run() invocation. Default is 0.\n\nproperty logid#\n\nTo identify logs generated by a particular Run() invocation.\n\nproperty only_execute_path_to_fetches#\n\nOnly execute the nodes needed by fetch list\n\nproperty terminate#\n\nSet to True to terminate any currently executing calls that are using this RunOptions instance. The individual calls will exit gracefully and return an error status.\n\nproperty training_mode#\n\nChoose to run in training or inferencing mode\n\n#### SessionOptions#\n\nclass onnxruntime.SessionOptions(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.SessionOptions)#\n\nConfiguration information for a session.\n\nadd_external_initializers(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.SessionOptions, arg0: [list](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#list), arg1: [list](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#list)) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)# add_free_dimension_override_by_denotation(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.SessionOptions, arg0: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str), arg1: [int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int)) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\nSpecify the dimension size for each denotation associated with an input’s free dimension.\n\nadd_free_dimension_override_by_name(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.SessionOptions, arg0: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str), arg1: [int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int)) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\nSpecify values of named dimensions within model inputs.\n\nadd_initializer(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.SessionOptions, arg0: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str), arg1: [object](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#object)) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)# add_session_config_entry(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.SessionOptions, arg0: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str), arg1: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\nSet a single session configuration entry as a pair of strings.\n\nproperty enable_cpu_mem_arena#\n\nEnables the memory arena on CPU. Arena may pre-allocate memory for future usage. Set this option to false if you don’t want it. Default is True.\n\nproperty enable_mem_pattern#\n\nEnable the memory pattern optimization. Default is true.\n\nproperty enable_mem_reuse#\n\nEnable the memory reuse optimization. Default is true.\n\nproperty enable_profiling#\n\nEnable profiling for this session. Default is false.\n\nproperty execution_mode#\n\nSets the execution mode. Default is sequential.\n\nproperty execution_order#\n\nSets the execution order. Default is basic topological order.\n\nget_session_config_entry(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.SessionOptions, arg0: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)) → [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)#\n\nGet a single session configuration value using the given configuration key.\n\nproperty graph_optimization_level#\n\nGraph optimization level for this session.\n\nproperty inter_op_num_threads#\n\nSets the number of threads used to parallelize the execution of the graph (across nodes). Default is 0 to let onnxruntime choose.\n\nproperty intra_op_num_threads#\n\nSets the number of threads used to parallelize the execution within nodes. Default is 0 to let onnxruntime choose.\n\nproperty log_severity_level#\n\nLog severity level. Applies to session load, initialization, etc. 0:Verbose, 1:Info, 2:Warning. 3:Error, 4:Fatal. Default is 2.\n\nproperty log_verbosity_level#\n\nVLOG level if DEBUG build and session_log_severity_level is 0. Applies to session load, initialization, etc. Default is 0.\n\nproperty logid#\n\nLogger id to use for session output.\n\nproperty optimized_model_filepath#\n\nFile path to serialize optimized model to. Optimized model is not serialized unless optimized_model_filepath is set. Serialized model format will default to ONNX unless: - add_session_config_entry is used to set ‘session.save_model_format’ to ‘ORT’, or - there is no ‘session.save_model_format’ config entry and optimized_model_filepath ends in ‘.ort’ (case insensitive)\n\nproperty profile_file_prefix#\n\nThe prefix of the profile file. The current time will be appended to the file name.\n\nregister_custom_ops_library(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.SessionOptions, arg0: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\nSpecify the path to the shared library containing the custom op kernels required to run a model.\n\nproperty use_deterministic_compute#\n\nWhether to use deterministic compute. Default is false.\n\nclass onnxruntime.ExecutionMode(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.ExecutionMode, value: [int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int))#\n\nMembers:\n\nORT_SEQUENTIAL\n\nORT_PARALLEL\n\nproperty name# class onnxruntime.ExecutionOrder(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.ExecutionOrder, value: [int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int))#\n\nMembers:\n\nDEFAULT\n\nPRIORITY_BASED\n\nMEMORY_EFFICIENT\n\nproperty name# class onnxruntime.GraphOptimizationLevel(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.GraphOptimizationLevel, value: [int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int))#\n\nMembers:\n\nORT_DISABLE_ALL\n\nORT_ENABLE_BASIC\n\nORT_ENABLE_EXTENDED\n\nORT_ENABLE_ALL\n\nproperty name# class onnxruntime.OrtAllocatorType(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.OrtAllocatorType, value: [int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int))#\n\nMembers:\n\nINVALID\n\nORT_DEVICE_ALLOCATOR\n\nORT_ARENA_ALLOCATOR\n\nproperty name# class onnxruntime.OrtArenaCfg(*args, **kwargs)#\n\nOverloaded function.\n\n__init__(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.OrtArenaCfg, arg0: int, arg1: int, arg2: int, arg3: int) -\u003e None\n\n__init__(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.OrtArenaCfg, arg0: dict) -\u003e None\n\nclass onnxruntime.OrtMemoryInfo(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.OrtMemoryInfo, arg0: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str), arg1: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.OrtAllocatorType, arg2: [int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int), arg3: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.OrtMemType)# class onnxruntime.OrtMemType(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.OrtMemType, value: [int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int))#\n\nMembers:\n\nCPU_INPUT\n\nCPU_OUTPUT\n\nCPU\n\nDEFAULT\n\nproperty name#\n\n### Functions#\n\n#### Allocators#\n\nonnxruntime.create_and_register_allocator(arg0: OrtMemoryInfo, arg1: OrtArenaCfg) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)# onnxruntime.create_and_register_allocator_v2(arg0: [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str), arg1: OrtMemoryInfo, arg2: [dict](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict) [[str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str), [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)], arg3: OrtArenaCfg) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\n#### Telemetry events#\n\nonnxruntime.disable_telemetry_events() → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\nDisables platform-specific telemetry collection.\n\nonnxruntime.enable_telemetry_events() → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\nEnables platform-specific telemetry collection where applicable.\n\n#### Providers#\n\nonnxruntime.get_all_providers() → [list](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#list) [[str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)]#\n\nReturn list of Execution Providers that this version of Onnxruntime can support. The order of elements represents the default priority order of Execution Providers from highest to lowest.\n\nonnxruntime.get_available_providers() → [list](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#list) [[str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)]#\n\nReturn list of available Execution Providers in this installed version of Onnxruntime. The order of elements represents the default priority order of Execution Providers from highest to lowest.\n\n#### Build, Version#\n\nonnxruntime.get_build_info() → [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)# onnxruntime.get_version_string() → [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)# onnxruntime.has_collective_ops() → [bool](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#bool)#\n\n#### Device#\n\nonnxruntime.get_device() → [str](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str)#\n\nReturn the device used to compute the prediction (CPU, MKL, …)\n\n#### Logging#\n\nonnxruntime.set_default_logger_severity(arg0: [int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int)) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\nSets the default logging severity. 0:Verbose, 1:Info, 2:Warning, 3:Error, 4:Fatal\n\nonnxruntime.set_default_logger_verbosity(arg0: [int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int)) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\nSets the default logging verbosity level. To activate the verbose log, you need to set the default logging severity to 0:Verbose level.\n\n#### Random#\n\nonnxruntime.set_seed(arg0: [int](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#int)) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\nSets the seed used for random number generation in Onnxruntime.\n\n### Data#\n\n#### OrtValue#\n\nclass onnxruntime.OrtValue(ortvalue, numpy_obj=None) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue)#\n\nA data structure that supports all ONNX data formats (tensors and non-tensors) that allows users to place the data backing these on a device, for example, on a CUDA supported device. This class provides APIs to construct and deal with OrtValues.\n\nas_sparse_tensor() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.as_sparse_tensor)#\n\nThe function will return SparseTensor contained in this OrtValue\n\ndata_ptr() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.data_ptr)#\n\nReturns the address of the first element in the OrtValue’s data buffer\n\ndata_type() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.data_type)#\n\nReturns the data type of the data in the OrtValue\n\ndevice_name() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.device_name)#\n\nReturns the name of the device where the OrtValue’s data buffer resides e.g. cpu, cuda, cann\n\nelement_type() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.element_type)#\n\nReturns the proto type of the data in the OrtValue if the OrtValue is a tensor.\n\nhas_value() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.has_value)#\n\nReturns True if the OrtValue corresponding to an optional type contains data, else returns False\n\nis_sparse_tensor() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.is_sparse_tensor)#\n\nReturns True if the OrtValue contains a SparseTensor, else returns False\n\nis_tensor() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.is_tensor)#\n\nReturns True if the OrtValue contains a Tensor, else returns False\n\nis_tensor_sequence() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.is_tensor_sequence)#\n\nReturns True if the OrtValue contains a Tensor Sequence, else returns False\n\nnumpy() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.numpy)#\n\nReturns a Numpy object from the OrtValue. Valid only for OrtValues holding Tensors. Throws for OrtValues holding non-Tensors. Use accessors to gain a reference to non-Tensor objects such as SparseTensor\n\nstatic ort_value_from_sparse_tensor(sparse_tensor) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.ort_value_from_sparse_tensor)#\n\nThe function will construct an OrtValue instance from a valid SparseTensor The new instance of OrtValue will assume the ownership of sparse_tensor\n\nstatic ortvalue_from_numpy(numpy_obj, device_type='cpu', device_id=0) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.ortvalue_from_numpy)#\n\nFactory method to construct an OrtValue (which holds a Tensor) from a given Numpy object A copy of the data in the Numpy object is held by the OrtValue only if the device is NOT cpu\n\nParameters:\n\nnumpy_obj – The Numpy object to construct the OrtValue from\n\ndevice_type – e.g. cpu, cuda, cann, cpu by default\n\ndevice_id – device id, e.g. 0\n\nstatic ortvalue_from_shape_and_type(shape=None, element_type=None, device_type='cpu', device_id=0) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.ortvalue_from_shape_and_type)#\n\nFactory method to construct an OrtValue (which holds a Tensor) from given shape and element_type\n\nParameters:\n\nshape – List of integers indicating the shape of the OrtValue\n\nelement_type – The data type of the elements in the OrtValue (numpy type)\n\ndevice_type – e.g. cpu, cuda, cann, cpu by default\n\ndevice_id – device id, e.g. 0\n\nshape() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.shape)#\n\nReturns the shape of the data in the OrtValue\n\nupdate_inplace(np_arr) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtValue.update_inplace)#\n\nUpdate the OrtValue in place with a new Numpy array. The numpy contents are copied over to the device memory backing the OrtValue. It can be used to update the input valuess for an InferenceSession with CUDA graph enabled or other scenarios where the OrtValue needs to be updated while the memory address can not be changed.\n\n#### SparseTensor#\n\nclass onnxruntime.SparseTensor(sparse_tensor) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor)#\n\nA data structure that project the C++ SparseTensor object The class provides API to work with the object. Depending on the format, the class will hold more than one buffer depending on the format\n\nInternal constructor\n\nas_blocksparse_view() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor.as_blocksparse_view)#\n\nThe method will return coo representation of the sparse tensor which will enable querying BlockSparse indices. If the instance did not contain BlockSparse format, it would throw. You can query coo indices as:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nblock_sparse_indices = sparse_tensor.as_blocksparse_view().indices()\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nwhich will return a numpy array that is backed by the native memory\n\nas_coo_view() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor.as_coo_view)#\n\nThe method will return coo representation of the sparse tensor which will enable querying COO indices. If the instance did not contain COO format, it would throw. You can query coo indices as:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\ncoo_indices = sparse_tensor.as_coo_view().indices()\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nwhich will return a numpy array that is backed by the native memory.\n\nas_csrc_view() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor.as_csrc_view)#\n\nThe method will return CSR(C) representation of the sparse tensor which will enable querying CRS(C) indices. If the instance dit not contain CSR(C) format, it would throw. You can query indices as:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\ninner_ndices = sparse_tensor.as_csrc_view().inner()\nouter_ndices = sparse_tensor.as_csrc_view().outer()\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nreturning numpy arrays backed by the native memory.\n\ndata_type() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor.data_type)#\n\nReturns a string data type of the data in the OrtValue\n\ndense_shape() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor.dense_shape)#\n\nReturns a numpy array(int64) containing a dense shape of a sparse tensor\n\ndevice_name() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor.device_name)#\n\nReturns the name of the device where the SparseTensor data buffers reside e.g. cpu, cuda\n\nformat() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor.format)#\n\nReturns a OrtSparseFormat enumeration\n\nstatic sparse_coo_from_numpy(dense_shape, values, coo_indices, ort_device) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor.sparse_coo_from_numpy)#\n\nFactory method to construct a SparseTensor in COO format from given arguments\n\nParameters:\n\ndense_shape – 1-D numpy array(int64) or a python list that contains a dense_shape of the sparse tensor must be on cpu memory\n\nvalues – a homogeneous, contiguous 1-D numpy array that contains non-zero elements of the tensor of a type.\n\ncoo_indices – contiguous numpy array(int64) that contains COO indices for the tensor. coo_indices may have a 1-D shape when it contains a linear index of non-zero values and its length must be equal to that of the values. It can also be of 2-D shape, in which has it contains pairs of coordinates for each of the nnz values and its length must be exactly twice of the values length.\n\nort_device –\n\ndescribes the backing memory owned by the supplied nummpy arrays. Only CPU memory is\n\nsuppored for non-numeric data types.\n\nFor primitive types, the method will map values and coo_indices arrays into native memory and will use them as backing storage. It will increment the reference count for numpy arrays and it will decrement it on GC. The buffers may reside in any storage either CPU or GPU. For strings and objects, it will create a copy of the arrays in CPU memory as ORT does not support those on other devices and their memory can not be mapped.\n\nstatic sparse_csr_from_numpy(dense_shape, values, inner_indices, outer_indices, ort_device) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor.sparse_csr_from_numpy)#\n\nFactory method to construct a SparseTensor in CSR format from given arguments\n\nParameters:\n\ndense_shape – 1-D numpy array(int64) or a python list that contains a dense_shape of the sparse tensor (rows, cols) must be on cpu memory\n\nvalues – a contiguous, homogeneous 1-D numpy array that contains non-zero elements of the tensor of a type.\n\ninner_indices – contiguous 1-D numpy array(int64) that contains CSR inner indices for the tensor. Its length must be equal to that of the values.\n\nouter_indices – contiguous 1-D numpy array(int64) that contains CSR outer indices for the tensor. Its length must be equal to the number of rows + 1.\n\nort_device –\n\ndescribes the backing memory owned by the supplied nummpy arrays. Only CPU memory is\n\nsuppored for non-numeric data types.\n\nFor primitive types, the method will map values and indices arrays into native memory and will use them as backing storage. It will increment the reference count and it will decrement then count when it is GCed. The buffers may reside in any storage either CPU or GPU. For strings and objects, it will create a copy of the arrays in CPU memory as ORT does not support those on other devices and their memory can not be mapped.\n\nto_cuda(ort_device) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor.to_cuda)#\n\nReturns a copy of this instance on the specified cuda device\n\nParameters:\n\nort_device – with name ‘cuda’ and valid gpu device id\n\nThe method will throw if:\n\nthis instance contains strings\n\nthis instance is already on GPU. Cross GPU copy is not supported\n\nCUDA is not present in this build\n\nif the specified device is not valid\n\nvalues() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#SparseTensor.values)#\n\nThe method returns a numpy array that is backed by the native memory if the data type is numeric. Otherwise, the returned numpy array that contains copies of the strings.\n\n### Devices#\n\n#### IOBinding#\n\nclass onnxruntime.IOBinding(session: Session) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#IOBinding)#\n\nThis class provides API to bind input/output to a specified device, e.g. GPU.\n\nbind_cpu_input(name, arr_on_cpu) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#IOBinding.bind_cpu_input)#\n\nbind an input to array on CPU :param name: input name :param arr_on_cpu: input values as a python array on CPU\n\nbind_input(name, device_type, device_id, element_type, shape, buffer_ptr) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#IOBinding.bind_input)# Parameters:\n\nname – input name\n\ndevice_type – e.g. cpu, cuda, cann\n\ndevice_id – device id, e.g. 0\n\nelement_type – input element type\n\nshape – input shape\n\nbuffer_ptr – memory pointer to input data\n\nbind_ortvalue_input(name, ortvalue) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#IOBinding.bind_ortvalue_input)# Parameters:\n\nname – input name\n\nortvalue – OrtValue instance to bind\n\nbind_ortvalue_output(name, ortvalue) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#IOBinding.bind_ortvalue_output)# Parameters:\n\nname – output name\n\nortvalue – OrtValue instance to bind\n\nbind_output(name, device_type='cpu', device_id=0, element_type=None, shape=None, buffer_ptr=None) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#IOBinding.bind_output)# Parameters:\n\nname – output name\n\ndevice_type – e.g. cpu, cuda, cann, cpu by default\n\ndevice_id – device id, e.g. 0\n\nelement_type – output element type\n\nshape – output shape\n\nbuffer_ptr – memory pointer to output data\n\ncopy_outputs_to_cpu() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#IOBinding.copy_outputs_to_cpu)#\n\nCopy output contents to CPU.\n\nget_outputs() [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#IOBinding.get_outputs)#\n\nReturns the output OrtValues from the Run() that preceded the call. The data buffer of the obtained OrtValues may not reside on CPU memory\n\nclass onnxruntime.SessionIOBinding(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.SessionIOBinding, arg0: arg0: str, arg1: object) -\u003e None\n\nbind_input(self: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.SessionIOBinding, arg0: str, arg1: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.OrtDevice, arg2: onnxruntime.capi.onnxruntime_pybind11_state.SessionIOBinding) → [None](https://docs.python.org/3/library/constants.html#None)#\n\n#### OrtDevice#\n\nclass onnxruntime.OrtDevice(c_ort_device) [[source]](https://onnxruntime.ai/docs/api/python/modules/onnxruntime/capi/onnxruntime_inference_collection.html#OrtDevice)#\n\nA data structure that exposes the underlying C++ OrtDevice\n\nInternal constructor\n\n### Internal classes#\n\nThese classes cannot be instantiated by users but they are returned by methods or functions of this library.\n\n#### ModelMetadata#\n\nclass onnxruntime.ModelMetadata#\n\nPre-defined and custom metadata about the model. It is usually used to identify the model used to run the prediction and facilitate the comparison.\n\nproperty custom_metadata_map#\n\nadditional metadata\n\nproperty description#\n\ndescription of the model\n\nproperty domain#\n\nONNX domain\n\nproperty graph_description#\n\ndescription of the graph hosted in the model\n\nproperty graph_name#\n\ngraph name\n\nproperty producer_name#\n\nproducer name\n\nproperty version#\n\nversion of the model\n\n#### NodeArg#\n\nclass onnxruntime.NodeArg#\n\nNode argument definition, for both input and output, including arg name, arg type (contains both type and shape).\n\nproperty name#\n\nnode name\n\nproperty shape#\n\nnode shape (assuming the node holds a tensor)\n\nproperty type#\n\nnode type\n\n## Backend#\n\nIn addition to the regular API which is optimized for performance and usability, ONNX Runtime also implements the [ONNX backend API](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/main/docs/ImplementingAnOnnxBackend.md) for verification of ONNX specification conformance. The following functions are supported:\n\nonnxruntime.backend.is_compatible(model, device=None, **kwargs)#\n\nReturn whether the model is compatible with the backend.\n\nParameters:\n\nmodel – unused\n\ndevice – None to use the default device or a string (ex: ‘CPU’)\n\nReturns:\n\nboolean\n\nonnxruntime.backend.prepare(model, device=None, **kwargs)#\n\nLoad the model and creates a&#96;onnxruntime.InferenceSession&#96; ready to be used as a backend.\n\nParameters:\n\nmodel – ModelProto (returned by onnx.load), string for a filename or bytes for a serialized model\n\ndevice – requested device for the computation, None means the default one which depends on the compilation settings\n\nkwargs – see&#96;onnxruntime.SessionOptions&#96;\n\nReturns:\n\n&#96;onnxruntime.InferenceSession&#96;\n\nonnxruntime.backend.run(model, inputs, device=None, **kwargs)#\n\nCompute the prediction.\n\nParameters:\n\nmodel –&#96;onnxruntime.InferenceSession&#96; returned by function prepare\n\ninputs – inputs\n\ndevice – requested device for the computation, None means the default one which depends on the compilation settings\n\nkwargs – see&#96;onnxruntime.RunOptions&#96;\n\nReturns:\n\npredictions\n\nonnxruntime.backend.supports_device(device)#\n\nCheck whether the backend is compiled with particular device support. In particular it’s used in the testing suite.",

[Services | Docker Docs](https://docs.docker.com/compose/compose-file/05-services/)\n4. [Container Device Interface (CDI) | Docker Docs](https://docs.docker.com/build/building/cdi)\n5. [Use Model Runner | Docker Docs](https://docs.docker.com/compose/bridge/use-model-runner/)\n\n# Docker Compose GPU Support with NVIDIA Container Toolkit\n\n## Prerequisites\n\nTo enable GPU support in Docker Compose, you must first install the NVIDIA drivers and the NVIDIA Container Toolkit.[2] The Docker Daemon must be configured accordingly.[1]\n\n## Configuring GPU Access in Compose\n\nGPUs are defined in the &#96;compose.yaml&#96; file using the &#96;device&#96; attribute within the &#96;deploy.resources.reservations&#96; section of services that need them.[1]\n\n### Required and Optional Device Properties\n\n- **&#96;capabilities&#96;** (required): Must be set as a list, e.g., &#96;capabilities: [gpu]&#96;. This field is mandatory or deployment will error.[1]\n- **&#96;driver&#96;**: Specified as a string, typically &#96;driver: 'nvidia'&#96;[1]\n- **&#96;count&#96;**: An integer or &#96;all&#96; representing the number of GPUs to reserve. If not specified, all available GPUs are used by default.[1]\n- **&#96;device_ids&#96;**: A list of GPU device IDs from the host (found via &#96;nvidia-smi&#96;). If not set, all GPUs are used by default.[1]\n- **&#96;options&#96;**: Key-value pairs for driver-specific options.[1]\n\n**Note:** &#96;count&#96; and &#96;device_ids&#96; are mutually exclusive—only define one at a time.[1]\n\n## Example\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\nservices:\n  test:\n    image: nvidia/cuda:12.9.0-base-ubuntu22.04\n    command: nvidia-smi\n    deploy:\n      resources:\n        reservations:\n          devices:\n            - driver: nvidia\n              count: 1\n              capabilities: [gpu]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nRun with &#96;docker compose up&#96; to deploy the service with GPU access.[1]",

{
  "markdown": "# Run Docker Compose services with GPU access\n\nCompose services can define GPU device reservations if the Docker host contains such devices and the Docker Daemon is set accordingly. For this, make sure you install the [prerequisites](https://docs.docker.com/engine/containers/resource_constraints/#gpu) if you haven't already done so.\n\nThe examples in the following sections focus specifically on providing service containers access to GPU devices with Docker Compose.\n\n## Enabling GPU access to service containers\n\nGPUs are referenced in a &#96;compose.yaml&#96; file using the [device](https://docs.docker.com/reference/compose-file/deploy/#devices) attribute from the Compose Deploy specification, within your services that need them.\n\nThis provides more granular control over a GPU reservation as custom values can be set for the following device properties:\n\n- &#96;capabilities&#96;. This value is specified as a list of strings. For example, &#96;capabilities: [gpu]&#96;. You must set this field in the Compose file. Otherwise, it returns an error on service deployment.\n- &#96;count&#96;. Specified as an integer or the value &#96;all&#96;, represents the number of GPU devices that should be reserved (providing the host holds that number of GPUs). If &#96;count&#96; is set to &#96;all&#96; or not specified, all GPUs available on the host are used by default.\n- &#96;device_ids&#96;. This value, specified as a list of strings, represents GPU device IDs from the host. You can find the device ID in the output of &#96;nvidia-smi&#96; on the host. If no &#96;device_ids&#96; are set, all GPUs available on the host are used by default.\n- &#96;driver&#96;. Specified as a string, for example &#96;driver: 'nvidia'&#96;\n- &#96;options&#96;. Key-value pairs representing driver specific options.\n\n\u003e [!IMPORTANT]\n\u003e \n\u003e You must set the &#96;capabilities&#96; field. Otherwise, it returns an error on service deployment.\n\n\u003e [!NOTE]\n\u003e \n\u003e &#96;count&#96; and &#96;device_ids&#96; are mutually exclusive. You must only define one field at a time.\n\nFor more information on these properties, see the [Compose Deploy Specification](https://docs.docker.com/reference/compose-file/deploy/#devices).\n\n### Example of a Compose file for running a service with access to 1 GPU device\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\nservices:\n  test:\n    image: nvidia/cuda:12.9.0-base-ubuntu22.04\n    command: nvidia-smi\n    deploy:\n      resources:\n        reservations:\n          devices:\n            - driver: nvidia\n              count: 1\n              capabilities: [gpu]\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nRun with Docker Compose:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;console\n$ docker compose up\nCreating network \"gpu_default\" with the default driver\nCreating gpu_test_1 ... done\nAttaching to gpu_test_1    \ntest_1  | +-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+\ntest_1  | | NVIDIA-SMI 450.80.02    Driver Version: 450.80.02    CUDA Version: 11.1     |\ntest_1  | |-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+\ntest_1  | | GPU  Name        Persistence-M| Bus-Id        Disp.A | Volatile Uncorr. ECC |\ntest_1  | | Fan  Temp  Perf  Pwr:Usage/Cap|         Memory-Usage | GPU-Util  Compute M. |\ntest_1  | |                               |                      |               MIG M. |\ntest_1  | |===============================+======================+======================|\ntest_1  | |   0  Tesla T4            On   | 00000000:00:1E.0 Off |                    0 |\ntest_1  | | N/A   23C    P8     9W /  70W |      0MiB / 15109MiB |      0%      Default |\ntest_1  | |                               |                      |                  N/A |\ntest_1  | +-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+\ntest_1  |                                                                                \ntest_1  | +-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+\ntest_1  | | Processes:                                                                  |\ntest_1  | |  GPU   GI   CI        PID   Type   Process name                  GPU Memory |\ntest_1  | |        ID   ID                                                   Usage      |\ntest_1  | |=============================================================================|\ntest_1  | |  No running processes found                                                 |\ntest_1  | +-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+\ngpu_test_1 exited with code 0\n\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nOn machines hosting multiple GPUs, the &#96;device_ids&#96; field can be set to target specific GPU devices and &#96;count&#96; can be used to limit the number of GPU devices assigned to a service container.\n\nYou can use &#96;count&#96; or &#96;device_ids&#96; in each of your service definitions. An error is returned if you try to combine both, specify an invalid device ID, or use a value of count that’s higher than the number of GPUs in your system.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;console\n$ nvidia-smi   \n+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+\n| NVIDIA-SMI 450.80.02    Driver Version: 450.80.02    CUDA Version: 11.0     |\n|-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+\n| GPU  Name        Persistence-M| Bus-Id        Disp.A | Volatile Uncorr. ECC |\n| Fan  Temp  Perf  Pwr:Usage/Cap|         Memory-Usage | GPU-Util  Compute M. |\n|                               |                      |               MIG M. |\n|===============================+======================+======================|\n|   0  Tesla T4            On   | 00000000:00:1B.0 Off |                    0 |\n| N/A   72C    P8    12W /  70W |      0MiB / 15109MiB |      0%      Default |\n|                               |                      |                  N/A |\n+-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+\n|   1  Tesla T4            On   | 00000000:00:1C.0 Off |                    0 |\n| N/A   67C    P8    11W /  70W |      0MiB / 15109MiB |      0%      Default |\n|                               |                      |                  N/A |\n+-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+\n|   2  Tesla T4            On   | 00000000:00:1D.0 Off |                    0 |\n| N/A   74C    P8    12W /  70W |      0MiB / 15109MiB |      0%      Default |\n|                               |                      |                  N/A |\n+-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+\n|   3  Tesla T4            On   | 00000000:00:1E.0 Off |                    0 |\n| N/A   62C    P8    11W /  70W |      0MiB / 15109MiB |      0%      Default |\n|                               |                      |                  N/A |\n+-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Access specific devices\n\nTo allow access only to GPU-0 and GPU-3 devices:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\nservices:\n  test:\n    image: tensorflow/tensorflow:latest-gpu\n    command: python -c \"import tensorflow as tf;tf.test.gpu_device_name()\"\n    deploy:\n      resources:\n        reservations:\n          devices:\n          - driver: nvidia\n            device_ids: ['0', '3']\n            capabilities: [gpu]\n\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;",

}
